cataloging_subject.label: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume III: Miscellaneous Works
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-level compilation represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00031: Thomas Arnold's 1871 third volume, which selects and edits thirty-three Middle English religious and polemical texts then ascribed to John Wyclif, together with encoded title matter, manuscript and bibliographic lists, contents, editorial headnotes, appendix, glossary, and a three-volume index. This record does not collapse the compilation, its constituent medieval works, their disputed and multiple authorships, the physical manuscripts, Arnold's complete printed volume, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one abstract medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume III: Miscellaneous Works
catalog_summary.author: John Wyclif, traditionally attributed; Arnold identifies Rolle-derived material and anonymous Wycliffite or Lollard writing, and several attributions are disputed or rejected
catalog_summary.date: Individual medieval composition dates remain under review; no. XXIX dates to 1388–1389; edition published 1871

titles.value: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume III: Miscellaneous Works
Select English Works of John Wyclif. Edited from Original MSS. by Thomas Arnold, M.A. Volume III. Miscellaneous Works
Select English Works of John Wyclif, Vol. III

notes: Arnold selected thirty-two of Shirley's sixty-five English works and part of another, organized them in three parts, supplied headnotes and reference matter, and excluded items he considered unsuitable or inauthentic.

scope.description: The thirty-three numbered medieval selections as presented by Arnold

notes: The volume title preserves the historical attribution, but Arnold himself rejects Wyclif's authorship of no. XXIX and questions several other items; current Parker cataloging describes the principal CCCC 296 collection as multi-author.

scope.description: The Richard Rolle core of the first seven canticle commentaries within no. I

notes: Arnold distinguishes a Rolle-derived core, Lollard retouching, and five additional canticle commentaries; this is not whole-item authorship. The exact authorial layering requires modern specialist review.

scope.description: Representative texts or textual layers treated as works of Wyclif's followers: the additions to no. I, posthumous no. XXIX, and the amplified vernacular material in no. XXXII

notes: Arnold states that no. IV was set from Brock's accurate transcript of Harley MS 2398; Arnold did not inspect that codex directly. The edition supplies only the surname, so this record does not silently expand it to an unverified personal identity.
Furnivall supplied Arnold with a useful collation of the Corser manuscript, now John Rylands English MS 90, against Harley MS 2398.
Arnold says Wright revised the careful CCCC MS 296 transcript used for no. XIV.
Arnold says Wright examined the Cambridge transcript against CCCC MS 296 before it was sent to him.
Arnold identifies Mr T. French as the transcriber of the TCD MS 244 and MS 245 texts used for nos. XXI and XXVI; the source does not expand his name further.

date_statements.display: Arnold's third volume was published in 1871
notes: The three-volume series spans 1869–1871; 1871 is the title-page date of volume III and the date of this edition-level cataloging subject.

date_statements.display: No. XXIX, On the Twenty-Five Articles, was composed in 1388 or 1389
notes: Arnold derives the terminus from Knighton's 1388 list and the reference to Pope Urban VI, who died in 1389; the date excludes Wyclif, who died in 1384, as author.

scope.description: The Middle English religious, polemical, and documentary texts

notes: This broad cultural origin does not transfer the production place of any one surviving witness to the composition of every text.
Oxford is the title-page place of publication. This statement does not claim that all compilation or editorial labor occurred there, and it is not the composition place of every medieval item.

scope.description: Editorial English occurs in title matter, lists, contents, headnotes, appendix, glossary, and index throughout the compilation
Latin occurs as embedded titles, quoted authorities, formulas, and passages throughout the compilation

form_statements.description: Prose compilation. No. XI alone contains one direct verse group of three lines; the XML's other five LG and thirty-eight L elements occur in editorial NOTE or HEADNOTE matter and do not make their numbered works mixed-form.

subject_statements.label: Church reform, clerical authority, and religious orders
Authorship, attribution, and the construction of the Wycliffite corpus

summaries.value: Arnold's third volume gathers thirty-three Middle English texts in three editorial parts: fifteen exegetical and didactic selections, fourteen controversial works, and four letters or documents. It preserves a historically important but unstable Wyclif attribution: Arnold rejects Wyclif's authorship of the post-1384 no. XXIX, recognizes Rolle material in no. I, and repeatedly qualifies other ascriptions. The encoded compilation also contains the edition title page, manuscript and Wyclif-literature lists, a classified list of works ascribed to Wyclif, contents, headnotes and notes, an appendix on Knighton's Chronicle, glossary, and combined index to all three volumes.

CME00031 encodes the edition title page, printed pp. xiii–xxiii, all three part title leaves and printed pp. 1–545 through the final index. It omits Arnold's Introduction on printed pp. v–xi and the physical scan's unprinted binding, blanks, flyleaves, library marks, and other copy-specific matter.

extent.description: Thirty-three numbered medieval selections in three parts, plus title matter, four front-matter lists or contents units, appendix, glossary, and index. The XML contains 11 DIV1, 38 DIV2, 219 DIV3, 1,201 P, 1,380 NOTE, 561 PB, 6 LG, and 41 L elements. Only one LG with three L children belongs directly to a medieval selection (no. XI); four LG and 31 L are inside NOTE, and one LG and seven L are inside HEADNOTE. No. X occupies two consecutive DIV2 units under one Roman numeral; nos. VI–VIII have an editorial wrapper followed by distinct divisions.

content_parts.label: Encoded title and front matter
content_parts.summary: Edition title page; list of manuscripts; bibliography of Wyclif literature; classified list of English works ascribed to Wyclif; and contents.

content_parts.label: Part I. Exegetical and Didactic Treatises
content_parts.summary: Fifteen numbered selections on canticles, basic prayers and doctrine, senses, sins, mercy, love, scripture, marriage, clerical support, and rules of life.

content_parts.label: I. Super Cantica Sacra
content_parts.summary: Commentaries on twelve biblical canticles, combining a Rolle-derived core with Wycliffite or Lollard revision and additions.

content_parts.label: II. Þe Ten Comaundementis
content_parts.summary: Exposition of the Ten Commandments.

content_parts.label: III. The Pater Noster
content_parts.summary: A short exposition of the Lord's Prayer.

content_parts.label: IV. Þe Pater Noster
content_parts.summary: A longer vernacular exposition of the Lord's Prayer, printed from a transcript of Harley MS 2398 and collated with the Corser manuscript, now John Rylands English MS 90.

content_parts.label: V. Ave Maria
content_parts.summary: Exposition of the Ave Maria.

content_parts.label: VI. On the Apostles' Creed
content_parts.summary: A brief exposition of the Apostles' Creed.

content_parts.label: VII. On the Five Outer Wits
content_parts.summary: A brief moral exposition of the five external senses.

content_parts.label: VIII. On the Five Inner Wits
content_parts.summary: A brief moral exposition of the five internal senses.

content_parts.label: IX. On the Seven Deadly Sins
content_parts.summary: An extended moral treatment of the seven deadly sins.

content_parts.label: X. Þe Seven Werkys of Mercy Bodyly and Gostly
content_parts.summary: Companion expositions of the seven corporal and seven spiritual works of mercy, encoded in two consecutive divisions under one numeral.

content_parts.label: XI. Five Questions on Love
content_parts.summary: Five questions concerning Christian love.

content_parts.label: XII. On the Sufficiency of Holy Scripture
content_parts.summary: A short argument for the sufficiency and authority of scripture.

content_parts.label: XIII. Of Weddid Men and Wifis and of Here Children Also
content_parts.summary: Pastoral instruction on marriage, spouses, and children.

content_parts.label: XIV. De Stipendiis Ministrorum
content_parts.summary: On how priests should be supported.

content_parts.label: XV. A Schort Reule of Lif
content_parts.summary: A short rule of life for people generally and for priests, lords, and laborers in particular.

content_parts.label: Part II. Controversial Works
content_parts.summary: Fourteen polemical selections on simony, clerical property and conduct, prayer, schism, excommunication, friars, apostasy, heresy, and Lollard defenses.

content_parts.label: XVI. Simonists and Apostates
content_parts.summary: A short polemic against simonists and apostates.

content_parts.label: XVII. Church Temporalities
content_parts.summary: On the church's temporal property and lordship.

content_parts.label: XVIII. De Precationibus Sacris
content_parts.summary: A treatise on sacred prayer.

content_parts.label: XIX. Lincolniensis
content_parts.summary: A short anti-fraternal tract framed by an attribution to Robert Grosseteste, bishop of Lincoln.

content_parts.label: XX. Vita Sacerdotum
content_parts.summary: A polemic on the proper life of priests.

content_parts.label: XXI. De Pontificum Romanorum Schismate
content_parts.summary: On the papal schism.

content_parts.label: XXII. The Grete Sentence of Curs Expouned
content_parts.summary: An extended exposition and critique of the great sentence of excommunication.

content_parts.label: XXIII. The Church and Her Members
content_parts.summary: A treatise on the church and its members.

content_parts.label: XXIV. Fifty Heresies and Errors of Friars
content_parts.summary: A numbered indictment of fifty alleged errors of the friars.

content_parts.label: XXV. De Blasphemia, Contra Fratres
content_parts.summary: A polemic against the friars concerning blasphemy.

content_parts.label: XXVI. De Apostasia Cleri
content_parts.summary: A polemic on clerical apostasy.

content_parts.label: XXVII. Seven Heresies
content_parts.summary: A treatment of seven alleged heresies.

content_parts.label: XXVIII. Octo in Quibus Seducuntur Simplices Christiani
content_parts.summary: Eight ways in which simple Christians are said to be deceived.

content_parts.label: XXIX. On the Twenty-Five Articles
content_parts.summary: A Lollard response to twenty-five charges, composed in 1388 or 1389 and therefore not by Wyclif.

content_parts.label: Part III. Letters and Documents
content_parts.summary: Four short eucharistic, epistolary, and parliamentary texts.

content_parts.label: XXX. Concerning the Eucharist
content_parts.summary: An English eucharistic confession transmitted in Knighton's Chronicle; Arnold prints it as connected with Wyclif, while later comparison treats its wording and responsibility cautiously.

content_parts.label: XXXI. Concerning the Eucharist
content_parts.summary: A second English eucharistic confession, edited from Bodley 647 and compared with Knighton's Chronicle.

content_parts.label: XXXII. Letter to Pope Urban
content_parts.summary: An English version or amplification of Wyclif's 1384 Latin letter to Pope Urban VI.

content_parts.label: XXXIII. A Petition to the King and Parliament
content_parts.summary: A Wycliffite petition arranged in four articles and addressed to the king and parliament.

content_parts.label: Appendix: On the Authenticity of the Fifth Book of Knyghton's Chronicle
content_parts.summary: Arnold's note on the authenticity and manuscript basis of the fifth book of Knighton's Chronicle.

content_parts.label: Glossary
content_parts.summary: Glossary to the Middle English texts.

content_parts.label: Index to Wyclif's Sermons and Miscellaneous Works
content_parts.summary: Combined index to the sermons and miscellaneous works across Arnold's three-volume edition.

related_works.title: Select English Works of John Wyclif (three-volume series)
notes: The cataloging subject is volume III; its final index covers the sermons and miscellaneous works across the series.

related_works.title: Chronicon of Henry Knighton
notes: Knighton's Chronicle transmits material used for nos. XXX–XXXI, supplies the list underlying no. XXIX's dating, and is the subject of Arnold's appendix. This relation does not identify the whole chronicle as a textual source for the compilation.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML for CME00031
evidence.summary: Establishes repository identity, compact source metadata, language declaration, and exact encoded text and apparatus.

evidence.citation: Structural and page-level inspection of CME00031 XML against Arnold's third volume
evidence.locator: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, VID; DIV1–DIV3 hierarchy; title and front matter; numbered selections I–XXXIII; appendix, glossary, and index; PB, P, NOTE, LG, and L counts
evidence.summary: Verifies the edition-level boundary, complete encoded content structure, title and page ranges, 33 numbered items, raw element counts, one mixed prose-and-verse item after separating editorial NOTE and HEADNOTE content, and omission of printed introduction pp. v–xi.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00031 item and About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
evidence.summary: Confirms item identity, title, attribution field, publication data, public access, and the 2016–2018 manually keyed and proofread production batch from public-domain editions.

evidence.citation: Thomas Arnold, ed., Select English Works of John Wyclif, vol. III, Miscellaneous Works (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1871), complete University of Michigan/Internet Archive scan
evidence.locator: title; introduction pp. v–xi; manuscript list pp. xiii–xvi; classified work list and contents pp. xvii–xxiii; texts pp. 1–523; appendix pp. 525–529; glossary pp. 530–538; index pp. 539–545
evidence.summary: Direct inspection verifies the complete edition, selection rationale, historical attribution cautions, manuscript sigla and headnotes, editorial method, all content and page boundaries, and the XML's omission of the introduction and copy-specific physical matter.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, Corpus Christi College Cambridge MS 296
evidence.summary: Confirms the principal Wycliffite manuscript's identity, late-14th-century context, contents and page ranges, and current cataloging judgment that the collection once wholly attributed to Wyclif contains works by multiple authors.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Digital Collections, John Rylands English MS 90
evidence.summary: Identifies Arnold's Corser manuscript as John Rylands English MS 90; confirms its end-14th-/early-15th-century date, complete 152-canvas viewer, and the no. IV Pater Noster on ff. 63r–65v.

evidence.citation: Margaret Aston, 'Wyclif and the Vernacular,' Studies in Church History Subsidia 5 (1987), 281–330
evidence.locator: especially the edition of the two eucharistic confessions and note 1
evidence.summary: Supports cautious attribution of English Wycliffite writing and gives the exact manuscript comparison for the eucharistic confessions: Bodley 647 f. 70r–v, Cotton Tiberius C VII ff. 183v–184v, and Cotton Claudius E III ff. 275v–276r.
Provides the entity, relation, witness, source-access, whole-object cache, rights, editorial-practice, and unresolved-question record underlying this work-level description.

open_questions.question: What is the current specialist attribution, date, and textual-layer model for each of the thirty-three numbered items?
next_steps: Review each tract against Anne Hudson's bibliography and subsequent item-level editions; retain Arnold's historical Wyclif umbrella only as a disputed attribution until that work is complete.

open_questions.question: Why does Arnold's front manuscript list assign no. XXIII to CCCC MS 296 when the no. XXIII headnote instead identifies Bodley 788 and Trinity College Dublin MS 245?
next_steps: Inspect CCCC 296 and modern tract handlists to determine whether the front-list reference is a typo, probably for no. XXVIII; preserve Bodley 788 as Arnold's directly stated base meanwhile.

open_questions.question: What item-level U-M production record explains retention of the title page and pp. xiii–545 while omitting Arnold's Introduction on pp. v–xi?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for selection, keying, and proofreading notes while preserving the directly observed source mapping.

notes: The volume title and inherited U-M author field are recorded as historical attribution evidence, not accepted as proof that Wyclif authored every medieval selection.
The record distinguishes medieval composition, physical witness production, Arnold's 1871 editorial compilation, U-M's 2016–2018 encoding batch, and the repository artifact.
Element totals and verse/prose counts are representation metrics from the XML, not newly normalized critical extents.
Witness identities and Arnold's use of them are modeled in the linked lineage manifest; a manuscript used only for collation is not automatically treated as the base of the printed text.
